concepts.title: Properties of pole and polar
concepts.conceptBody:
Pole and polar

If through a point $\text{P}$ (within or outside a Parabola) there be drawn any straight line to meet the parabola in $Q\And R$, then the locus of point of intersection of the tangents at $Q$ & $R$ is called the Polar of P; also P is called the Pole of the Polar.

The polar of a point $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$ with respect to the parabola ${{\text{y}}^{2}}=4\text{ax}$ is

$\text{y}{{\text{y}}_{1}}=2\text{a}\left( \text{x}+{{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)$

Note : 

  • When the point $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$ lies outside the parabola the equation to its polar is the same as the equation to the chord of contact of tangents drawn at $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$.
  • When $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$ is on the parabola the polar is the same as the tangent at the point.

If the polar of a point $\text{P}$ passes through the point $\text{T}$, then the polar of $\text{T}$ goes through P. 

Let $\text{P}$ be the point $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$ and $\text{T}$ the point $\left( \text{h},\text{k} \right)$.

The polar of $\text{P}$ is $\text{y}{{\text{y}}_{1}}=2\text{a}\left( \text{x}+{{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

Since, it passes, through $\text{T}$, we have

${{\text{y}}_{1}}\text{k}=2\text{a}\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}}+\text{h} \right)$

The polar of $\text{T}$ is, $\text{yk}=2\text{a}\left( \text{x}+\text{h} \right)\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$

Since, (1) is true, this equation is satisfied by the coordinates ${{x}_{1}}$ and ${{y}_{1}}$.

Hence, the proposition.

Cor. : The point of intersection, $\text{T}$, of the polars of two points, $\text{P}$ and $\text{Q}$, is the pole of the line $\text{PQ}$.

To find the pole of a given straight line with respect to the parabola. 

Let the given straight line be

$\text{Ax}+\text{By}+\text{C}=0$

If its pole be the point $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$, it must be the same straight line as,

$\text{y}{{\text{y}}_{1}}=2\text{a}\left( \text{x}+{{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)$

i.e., $2ax-y{{y}_{1}}+2a{{x}_{1}}=0$

Since, these straight lines are the same, we have

$\frac{2\text{a}}{\text{A}}=\frac{-{{\text{y}}_{1}}}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ B}}=\frac{2\text{a}{{\text{x}}_{1}}}{\text{C}}$

i.e., ${{\text{x}}_{1}}=\frac{\text{C}}{\text{A}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ and }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{y}}_{1}}=-\frac{2\text{Ba}}{\text{A}}$
